**Fenugreek**, or *Trigonella foenum-graecum*, is a plant used as herb and spice.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The seeds of the plant are light brown and resemble small bits of gravel.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Their characteristic flavor and aroma comes from sotolon,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) which is reminiscent of caramelized sugar and imitation maple flavor.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) They also have secondary notes that some describe as musty or mushroom-like.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The leaves are much milder in flavor than the seeds.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Fresh fenugreek leaves are perishable and should be stored in the fridge for a few days like other leafy greens. Dried fenugreek leaves keep well at room temperature. Fenugreek seeds keep for a very long time (months to years) at room temperature if kept in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Ground fenugreek seed has a shorter shelf life than whole—storage in the fridge can help.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Fenugreek is common in South Asian and Persian cuisines.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5) The leaves can be sautéed, stewed, incorporated into breads, and more.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:4-6) The seeds—especially roasted—are used as a spice, where they are incorporated into spice blends, chutneys, and curries.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:5-5)

Since they are so hard, the seeds must be ground in a specialized mortar or spice grinder.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)
